questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Surveying is best defined as the art and science of
options: determining the relative positions of points on, above or below the earth's surface
designing the ventilation network of a mine
estimating the calorific value of coal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Surveying fixes relative position.

**Reasoning.** It measures distances, directions and elevations to determine the relative positions of points and to represent them on plans/maps; in mining it links surface and underground workings.

**Answer.** Determining relative positions of points.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A 'plan' differs from a 'map' chiefly in that a plan is drawn to a
options: small scale covering a large area
large scale covering a small area, with little distortion
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scale distinguishes plans from maps.

**Reasoning.** A plan uses a large scale for a small area (e.g. a mine layout) showing fine detail; a map uses a small scale for a large area. Mine workings are shown on large-scale plans.

**Answer.** Large scale, small area.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In chain surveying, the main principle followed is
options: triangulation — dividing the area into well-conditioned triangles measured by chaining
measuring angles only with a theodolite
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Chain surveying works by triangles.

**Reasoning.** The area is covered by a framework of triangles whose sides are measured by chaining (a triangle is the only figure fixed by its sides alone); offsets locate detail. Well-conditioned (near-equilateral) triangles give the best accuracy.

**Answer.** Triangulation of chained sides.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A metric chain is 20 m long and divided into 100 links. The length of one link, in cm, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Link length $=\dfrac{\text{chain length}}{\text{number of links}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{20\ \text{m}}{100}=0.20\ \text{m}=20\ \text{cm}$$

**Answer.** 20 cm.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Ranging in chain surveying is the operation of
options: establishing intermediate points on a straight survey line between its end stations
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ranging keeps the chain on line.

**Reasoning.** Intermediate ranging rods are fixed in line with the two end stations so that chaining follows a straight line; it may be direct (ends intervisible) or indirect (reciprocal).

**Answer.** Fixing intermediate points on the line.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: An 'offset' in chain surveying is
options: the error in the chain length
the lateral distance of a detail point from the chain (survey) line
the difference of fore and back bearings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Offsets locate detail relative to the chain line.

**Reasoning.** Perpendicular (or oblique) offsets give the distance of features such as buildings or boundaries from the main line, so they can be plotted; perpendicular offsets are preferred for accuracy.

**Answer.** Lateral distance of a point from the chain line.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A line is measured as 500 m with a 20 m chain that is found to be 0.05 m too long. The corrected (true) length, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** True length $=$ measured $\times\dfrac{\text{actual chain length}}{\text{nominal length}}$.

**Working.**
$$L=500\times\frac{20.05}{20}=500\times1.0025=501.25\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 501.25 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The whole-circle bearing (WCB) of a line is measured
options: from north or south, between 0° and 90°
clockwise from the north, between 0° and 360°
as the vertical angle from the horizon
questions.solution:
**Concept.** WCB is a clockwise azimuth.

**Reasoning.** It is reckoned clockwise from the north meridian and can take any value from 0° to 360°; the prismatic compass reads it directly.

**Answer.** Clockwise from north, 0°–360°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A reduced (quadrantal) bearing is expressed
options: as an angle between 0° and 90° measured from north or south, with the quadrant indicated (NE, SE, SW, NW)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Quadrantal bearings reference the nearer meridian.

**Reasoning.** The angle is measured from north or south, toward east or west, lying between 0° and 90°, e.g. N30°E or S45°W; the surveyor's compass reads these.

**Answer.** 0°–90° from N/S with quadrant.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A line has a whole-circle bearing of 210°. Its reduced bearing is S θ W; the value of θ, in degrees, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** For WCB between 180° and 270° the RB is S(WCB−180°)W.

**Working.**
$$\theta=210-180=30^{\circ}\Rightarrow \text{S}30^{\circ}\text{W}$$

**Answer.** 30°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A line has a fore bearing of 45°. Its back bearing (WCB), in degrees, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Back bearing $=$ fore bearing $\pm180°$.

**Working.**
$$BB=45+180=225^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** 225°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Magnetic declination at a place is the
options: angle between the line of sight and the horizontal
horizontal angle between the true (geographic) north and the magnetic north
difference between fore and back bearings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The magnetic needle does not point to true north.

**Reasoning.** Declination is the horizontal angle between true north and the direction the compass needle takes (magnetic north); it varies with place and time and must be applied to convert magnetic to true bearings.

**Answer.** Angle between true north and magnetic north.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The magnetic bearing of a line is 130° and the magnetic declination is 2° East. The true bearing, in degrees, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** True bearing $=$ magnetic bearing $+$ easterly declination.

**Working.**
$$TB=130+2=132^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** 132°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Local attraction in compass surveying is detected when
options: the difference between the fore and back bearings of a line is not exactly 180°
the level bubble is off centre
the offset exceeds the tape length
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Magnetic disturbance deflects the needle.

**Reasoning.** Nearby iron/magnetic material pulls the needle, so the observed fore and back bearings of a line differ by something other than 180°; stations free of attraction (180° difference) are used to correct the rest.

**Answer.** FB and BB differ by ≠ 180°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: From a station the bearings of two lines are 60° and 150°. The included angle between the lines, in degrees, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Included angle $=$ difference of the two bearings.

**Working.**
$$150-60=90^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** 90°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a prismatic compass the graduated ring
options: is fixed to the box and rotates with the sight vane
is attached to the magnetic needle and is read through a prism, giving the whole-circle bearing directly
must be levelled with a plumb bob
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The card turns with the needle; the box turns about it.

**Reasoning.** The graduated ring is carried by the needle and stays oriented to magnetic north; the observer reads the WCB through the prism against the object vane, so readings are taken directly.

**Answer.** Ring on the needle, read by prism (WCB).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Levelling is the branch of surveying used to determine
options: the relative heights (elevations) of points and differences in level
the magnetic bearing of a line
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Levelling measures vertical position.

**Reasoning.** Using a level and staff it finds the elevation differences between points relative to a datum, essential for grades, contours, drainage and mine plans.

**Answer.** Relative heights/level differences.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A bench mark (BM) in levelling is
options: a temporary point that changes each day
a fixed reference point of known elevation relative to the datum (usually mean sea level)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bench marks anchor elevations.

**Reasoning.** A BM is a permanent point whose reduced level (RL) above the datum is known; levelling is referred to BMs so that all heights tie to a common datum such as MSL.

**Answer.** Fixed point of known elevation.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A backsight of 1.500 m is taken on a bench mark of RL 100.000 m. The height of instrument (HI), in m, is ____ (three decimals).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: With a height of instrument of 101.500 m, a foresight of 0.800 m is taken on a point. The reduced level of that point, in m, is ____ (one decimal).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A backsight (BS) reading in levelling is
options: the last reading before moving the instrument
the first staff reading taken on a point of known elevation after setting up the level
questions.solution:
**Concept.** BS ties the instrument to a known RL.

**Reasoning.** The first reading after setting up is taken on a point of known RL (BM or change point); adding it to that RL gives the HI.

**Answer.** First reading on a known point.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A foresight (FS) in levelling is
options: the first reading taken on a bench mark
the last staff reading taken from an instrument setting, just before the level is shifted
the magnetic bearing of the staff
the horizontal distance to the staff
questions.solution:
**Concept.** FS closes an instrument setting.

**Reasoning.** It is the final reading from a given setup (on a change point or final point) before moving the instrument; it is subtracted from the HI to obtain the RL.

**Answer.** Last reading before shifting the instrument.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Consecutive staff readings are 1.200 m (back station) and 1.700 m (forward station). The magnitude of the fall from the back to the forward station, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A larger forward reading means the ground has fallen.

**Working.**
$$1.700-1.200=0.500\ \text{m (fall)}$$

**Answer.** 0.5 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A dumpy (or automatic) level is an instrument used primarily for
options: establishing a horizontal line of sight to read a levelling staff and determine elevations
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A level provides a horizontal line of collimation.

**Reasoning.** The level's telescope, once set up, gives a horizontal sight; staff readings along it yield differences in elevation. An automatic level uses a compensator to keep the line horizontal.

**Answer.** Horizontal sighting for levelling.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a line of levels the sum of all backsights is 3.250 m and the sum of all foresights is 2.100 m. The difference between the last and first reduced levels (last − first), in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Arithmetic check: $\sum BS-\sum FS=RL_{last}-RL_{first}$.

**Working.**
$$3.250-2.100=1.150\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** +1.15 m (net rise).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A contour line on a plan is
options: a line of constant magnetic bearing
an imaginary line joining points of equal elevation (reduced level)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Contours show equal height.

**Reasoning.** Every point on a contour has the same RL; the pattern of contours depicts the shape of the ground (relief) on a plan.

**Answer.** Line of equal elevation.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The contour interval is the
options: horizontal distance between two adjacent contours
constant vertical distance (difference in elevation) between successive contours
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Interval is a vertical quantity.

**Reasoning.** It is the fixed elevation difference between consecutive contours (e.g. 1 m, 5 m); smaller intervals give more detail. The horizontal spacing is the 'horizontal equivalent'.

**Answer.** Vertical distance between contours.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Two adjacent contours differ by 5 m in elevation and are 50 m apart on the ground (horizontal). The ground slope, expressed as 1 in n, gives n = ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Slope $=\dfrac{\text{vertical}}{\text{horizontal}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{5}{50}=\frac{1}{10}\Rightarrow 1\text{ in }10$$

**Answer.** n = 10.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: On a contour plan, contours that are closely spaced indicate
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closeness reflects steepness.

**Reasoning.** For a fixed contour interval, closely spaced contours mean a large height change over a short horizontal distance, i.e. a steep slope; widely spaced contours indicate gentle ground.

**Answer.** Steep ground.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A 'grade contour' (contour gradient) marked on the ground is a line that
options: maintains a constant specified gradient (slope) along its length
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A grade line keeps slope constant, not height.

**Reasoning.** Used to lay out haul roads, drains and inclines, the grade contour rises (or falls) at a fixed rate; its plan position is found by stepping a fixed horizontal distance per contour interval.

**Answer.** Constant-gradient line.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A theodolite is primarily used to measure
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The theodolite is an angle-measuring instrument.

**Reasoning.** It reads horizontal and vertical angles on graduated circles; with stadia or EDM it can also give distances, making it central to traversing and triangulation.

**Answer.** Horizontal and vertical angles.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Observing a theodolite angle on both 'face left' and 'face right' and meaning the results
options: doubles the work for no benefit
eliminates instrumental errors such as line-of-collimation and trunnion-axis errors
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Reversing the telescope cancels symmetric errors.

**Reasoning.** Changing face reverses the sign of collimation and trunnion-axis (and horizontal-axis) errors, so their mean is free of these instrumental defects.

**Answer.** Eliminates instrumental errors.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A theodolite main scale is graduated to 20′ and its vernier has 60 divisions matching 59 main-scale divisions. The least count, in seconds, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Least count $=\dfrac{\text{smallest main division}}{\text{number of vernier divisions}}$.

**Working.**
$$LC=\frac{20'}{60}=\frac{20\times60''}{60}=20''$$

**Answer.** 20 seconds.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The temporary adjustments of a theodolite carried out at each station are
options: centring, levelling and focusing (elimination of parallax)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Temporary adjustments precede every observation.

**Reasoning.** The instrument is set over the station (centring), made level (levelling) and the eyepiece/objective focused to remove parallax before angles are read; permanent adjustments concern the instrument's geometry.

**Answer.** Centring, levelling, focusing.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A traverse in surveying is
options: a series of connected survey lines whose lengths and directions (or angles) are measured
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A traverse is a framework of measured lines.

**Reasoning.** Successive stations are connected by lines whose lengths and bearings (or included angles) are observed; coordinates of the stations are then computed.

**Answer.** Connected lines with measured length and direction.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The main advantage of a closed traverse over an open traverse is that
options: it provides a check on the field work because the misclosure can be computed and adjusted
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closure gives an internal check.

**Reasoning.** A closed traverse returns to its start (or to known points), so the angular and linear misclosure can be evaluated and distributed; an open traverse offers no such self-check.

**Answer.** Misclosure can be checked and adjusted.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A traverse line is 100 m long with a whole-circle bearing of 30°. Its latitude (N–S component), in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Latitude $=L\cos\theta$.

**Working.**
$$100\cos30^{\circ}=100\times0.8660=86.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 86.6 m (north).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For the same line (length 100 m, bearing 30°), its departure (E–W component), in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Departure $=L\sin\theta$.

**Working.**
$$100\sin30^{\circ}=100\times0.5=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 50 m (east).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In traverse computation, the 'latitude' and 'departure' of a line are its projections on the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** They resolve a line into rectangular components.

**Reasoning.** Latitude $=L\cos\theta$ is the N–S projection (N positive) and departure $=L\sin\theta$ is the E–W projection (E positive); summing them gives coordinate differences.

**Answer.** N–S and E–W axes.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a closed traverse the algebraic sum of latitudes is +0.30 m and the sum of departures is +0.40 m. The linear closing error, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closing error $=\sqrt{(\sum L)^2+(\sum D)^2}$.

**Working.**
$$e=\sqrt{0.30^2+0.40^2}=\sqrt{0.09+0.16}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 0.50 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The Bowditch (compass) rule for adjusting a traverse distributes the closing error in proportion to the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bowditch assumes errors proportional to length.

**Reasoning.** Correction to a line's latitude (or departure) $=$ total error $\times\dfrac{\text{line length}}{\text{perimeter}}$; it suits work where angular and linear precision are comparable.

**Answer.** Length of each line.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The transit rule for traverse adjustment distributes the closing error in proportion to the
options: magnitude of the latitude (or departure) of each line
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Transit weights by component magnitude.

**Reasoning.** Correction to a latitude $=$ total latitude error $\times\dfrac{|\text{latitude of line}|}{\sum|\text{latitudes}|}$ (similarly for departures); it is preferred when angular measurements are more precise than linear ones.

**Answer.** Magnitude of latitude/departure.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A closed traverse has a perimeter of 1000 m and a latitude misclosure of 0.20 m. Using Bowditch's rule, the latitude correction for a line of length 200 m, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Correction $=$ error $\times\dfrac{\text{line length}}{\text{perimeter}}$.

**Working.**
$$0.20\times\frac{200}{1000}=0.20\times0.2=0.04\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 0.04 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A closed traverse of perimeter 1000 m has a linear closing error of 0.50 m. Its relative error of closure expressed as 1 in n gives n = ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Relative error $=\dfrac{e}{\text{perimeter}}$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{0.50}{1000}=\frac{1}{2000}\Rightarrow 1\text{ in }2000$$

**Answer.** n = 2000.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Gale's traverse table is used to
options: compute the calorific value of coal
systematically compute latitudes, departures, corrections and independent coordinates of traverse stations
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gale's table organises traverse computation.

**Reasoning.** It tabulates each line's length, bearing, latitude, departure, corrections (Bowditch/transit) and the running (independent) coordinates, providing a clear computation and closure check.

**Answer.** Tabular traverse computation.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A station has coordinates N = 1000.0 m, E = 1000.0 m. A line to the next station has latitude +86.6 m and departure +50.0 m. The northing of the next station, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** New northing $=$ old northing $+$ latitude.

**Working.**
$$1000.0+86.6=1086.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 1086.6 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For the line in the previous problem (departure +50.0 m), the easting of the next station, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** New easting $=$ old easting $+$ departure.

**Working.**
$$1000.0+50.0=1050.0\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 1050.0 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For a perfectly balanced closed-loop traverse, the algebraic sums of latitudes and of departures should each equal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A loop returns to its origin.

**Reasoning.** Since the traverse closes on its starting point, the net north–south travel and the net east–west travel are both zero; non-zero sums are the misclosure to be adjusted.

**Answer.** Zero.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Tacheometry (stadia surveying) is used to determine
options: horizontal distances and elevations rapidly using the staff intercept between stadia hairs, without chaining
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stadia replaces chaining by optical distance.

**Reasoning.** The intercept of the staff between the upper and lower stadia hairs, combined with the vertical angle, gives both horizontal distance and difference in elevation; it is fast over rough ground.

**Answer.** Distances and elevations from staff intercept.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A tacheometer has multiplying constant K = 100 and additive constant C = 0. For a horizontal sight with a staff intercept of 0.50 m, the horizontal distance, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $D=Ks+C$ for a horizontal sight.

**Working.**
$$D=100\times0.50+0=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 50 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: With K = 100, C = 0 and a horizontal staff intercept of 1.20 m, the horizontal distance, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $D=Ks+C$.

**Working.**
$$D=100\times1.20=120\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 120 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For a modern internal-focusing tacheometer, the stadia constants are usually
options: both equal to the focal length
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Constants are set by the stadia hair spacing and optics.

**Reasoning.** The multiplying constant $K=f/i\approx100$; internal focusing makes the additive constant $C\approx0$, simplifying $D=Ks$.

**Answer.** K ≈ 100, C ≈ 0.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A stadia sight is inclined at 10° to the horizontal. With K = 100, C = 0 and staff intercept 1.00 m, the horizontal distance, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $D=Ks\cos^{2}\theta$ for an inclined sight.

**Working.**
$$D=100\times1.00\times\cos^{2}10^{\circ}=100\times0.9698=96.98\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 96.98 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For a simple circular curve, the tangent length, length of curve and apex (intersection) angle are related to the
options: radius R and the deflection (intersection) angle Δ
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Curve geometry depends on R and Δ.

**Reasoning.** Tangent length $T=R\tan(\Delta/2)$ and curve length $L=R\Delta_{rad}$; the deflection angle Δ between the two tangents and the radius R fully define the simple curve.

**Answer.** R and Δ.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A simple circular curve has radius 200 m and deflection angle 60°. The tangent length, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $T=R\tan(\Delta/2)$.

**Working.**
$$T=200\tan30^{\circ}=200\times0.5774=115.47\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 115.47 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For the same curve (R = 200 m, Δ = 60°), the length of the curve, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $L=R\,\Delta$ with Δ in radians.

**Working.**
$$L=200\times\frac{60\pi}{180}=200\times1.0472=209.44\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 209.44 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The 'degree of a curve' is defined as the angle subtended at the centre by a
options: standard arc (or chord) length such as 30 m
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Degree of curve is an alternative to radius.

**Reasoning.** It is the central angle for a standard arc (30 m in metric practice); a sharper curve (smaller R) has a larger degree. $R=\dfrac{1718.9}{D}$ for a 30 m arc.

**Answer.** Central angle for a standard arc/chord.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Using the 30 m arc definition, a curve of degree 4° has a radius, in m, of ____ (two decimals).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A transition (easement) curve is introduced between a straight and a circular curve mainly to
options: gradually change the curvature and introduce superelevation, avoiding sudden lateral acceleration
remove the need for a theodolite
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Curvature must not jump from zero to 1/R.

**Reasoning.** The transition curve gives a radius decreasing gradually from infinity to R, so centrifugal force and superelevation build up smoothly, improving comfort and safety on haul roads/rail.

**Answer.** Gradual curvature and superelevation.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Rankine's method of setting out a circular curve uses
options: offsets from the long chord only
tangential (deflection) angles measured with a theodolite and chord lengths taped on the ground
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Deflection angles locate successive chord points.

**Reasoning.** Each chord subtends a tangential angle $\delta=\dfrac{1718.9\,C}{R}$ minutes; the theodolite is set to cumulative deflection angles while the chord length is taped, fixing points on the curve.

**Answer.** Deflection (tangential) angle method.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The area of an irregular figure bounded by offsets taken at regular intervals along a base line is commonly found by
options: the trapezoidal rule or Simpson's rule
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Numerical integration of offsets gives area.

**Reasoning.** Treating the boundary between offsets as straight gives the trapezoidal rule; as a parabola gives Simpson's rule (more accurate for curved boundaries, needs even number of intervals).

**Answer.** Trapezoidal / Simpson's rule.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Offsets 0, 2, 4, 3, 0 m are taken at a regular interval of 10 m. By the trapezoidal rule the enclosed area, in m², is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $A=d\left[\dfrac{o_1+o_n}{2}+\sum o_{mid}\right]$.

**Working.**
$$A=10\left[\frac{0+0}{2}+(2+4+3)\right]=10\times9=90\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** 90 m².

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For the same offsets (0, 2, 4, 3, 0 m at 10 m interval), Simpson's rule gives an area, in m², of ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $A=\dfrac{d}{3}\left[o_1+o_n+4\sum o_{odd}+2\sum o_{even}\right]$.

**Working.**
$$A=\frac{10}{3}\big[(0+0)+4(2+3)+2(4)\big]=\frac{10}{3}(28)=93.33\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** 93.33 m².

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Simpson's one-third rule for area requires that the number of divisions (intervals) be
options: even, with equal spacing (i.e. an odd number of ordinates)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Parabolic fitting pairs the strips.

**Reasoning.** The rule fits a parabola over each pair of strips, so the number of intervals must be even (ordinates odd) and the spacing equal; otherwise the last strip is handled separately.

**Answer.** Even number of equal intervals.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A triangular plot has corner coordinates (0, 0), (40, 0) and (0, 30) m. Its area by the coordinate (cross-product) method, in m², is ____.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Two cross-sections 30 m apart have areas 20 m² and 40 m². By the average end-area (trapezoidal) method the volume, in m³, is ____.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Cross-sections at the two ends (30 m apart) have areas 20 m² and 40 m², and the mid-section area is 32 m². By the prismoidal formula the volume, in m³, is ____.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The prismoidal correction applied to an earthwork volume
options: increases the end-area volume to a larger value always
adjusts the (usually larger) end-area volume to the more accurate prismoidal volume
is the same as the closing error
questions.solution:
**Concept.** End-area overestimates for non-linear sections.

**Reasoning.** The trapezoidal (end-area) method generally gives a slightly larger volume than the true prismoid; the prismoidal correction is subtracted to obtain the accurate volume.

**Answer.** Refines end-area to prismoidal volume.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A gradient of '1 in 50' means the ground (or roadway)
options: rises or falls 1 unit vertically for every 50 units horizontally
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gradient is a vertical-to-horizontal ratio.

**Reasoning.** '1 in 50' = $\tfrac{1}{50}$ rise per unit horizontal distance ($=2\%$); steeper grades have smaller n.

**Answer.** 1 vertical per 50 horizontal.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A roadway rises 3 m over a horizontal distance of 150 m. Its gradient expressed as 1 in n gives n = ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $n=\dfrac{\text{horizontal}}{\text{vertical}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{150}{3}=50\Rightarrow 1\text{ in }50$$

**Answer.** n = 50.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A grade rises 4 m over 100 m horizontal. Expressed as a percentage, the gradient, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Percentage gradient $=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{horizontal}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{4}{100}\times100=4\%$$

**Answer.** 4 %.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In the figure, two straights meet at the intersection point I with deflection angle Δ, and a circular curve of radius R joins them at the tangent points T1 and T2. The distance from a tangent point to I is the

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='160' x2='160' y2='55' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='55' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M 70 160 A 150 150 0 0 1 250 160' fill='none' stroke='#1aa179' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='55' r='3' fill='#000'/><text x='150' y='45' font-size='12'>I</text><text x='40' y='175' font-size='12'>T1</text><text x='255' y='175' font-size='12'>T2</text><text x='120' y='195' font-size='11'>deflection angle Δ</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simple circular curve between two straights

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tangent length runs from tangent point to intersection.

**Reasoning.** $T1I=T2I=R\tan(\Delta/2)$ is the tangent length; the curve length is $R\Delta_{rad}$, the mid-ordinate and external distance are separate elements.

**Answer.** Tangent length $R\tan(\Delta/2)$.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Compared with surface surveying, underground mine surveying is more difficult chiefly because of
options: the absence of any need for accuracy
cramped workings, artificial lighting, short and steeply inclined sights, and stations fixed in the roof
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The working environment constrains observation.

**Reasoning.** Restricted space, poor light, dust, short/inclined lines of sight and roof-mounted stations all complicate underground work; orientation must also be carried down a shaft.

**Answer.** Confined, poorly lit conditions with roof stations.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A correlation (orientation) survey in mining is carried out to
options: measure the calorific value of coal
transfer coordinates and the bearing (orientation) from the surface survey down a shaft to the underground workings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Surface and underground surveys must share one framework.

**Reasoning.** Correlation establishes the position and azimuth of an underground line relative to the surface grid by transferring coordinates and bearing down the shaft (plumb wires, gyro-theodolite, etc.).

**Answer.** Transfer of coordinates and bearing down the shaft.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In the wire (plumb-line) method of shaft plumbing, two plumb wires are hung down the shaft mainly to
options: define a vertical plane whose direction can be transferred to fix the orientation of the underground survey
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Two wires define a reference direction underground.

**Reasoning.** The two suspended wires establish a vertical plane; its azimuth, known on the surface, is connected to an underground theodolite station (often via a Weisbach triangle) to orient the workings.

**Answer.** Establish a reference direction (vertical plane).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The Weisbach triangle method of shaft correlation places the theodolite
options: exactly on the line of the two plumb wires
slightly off the line joining the two wires, forming a long, narrow triangle with a very small angle at the instrument
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A small offset angle is measured precisely.

**Reasoning.** Because the instrument cannot sit exactly on the wire line, it is set a little off, forming a very acute triangle; the tiny angle at the instrument is computed by the sine rule to bring the underground line onto the wire-plane direction.

**Answer.** Slightly off the wire line (narrow triangle).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A gyro-theodolite gives the gyro azimuth of a reference line as 95°30′. The instrument calibration (E) correction is −0°30′. The true azimuth, in degrees, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** True azimuth $=$ gyro azimuth $+$ calibration correction.

**Working.**
$$95^{\circ}30'+(-0^{\circ}30')=95^{\circ}00'$$

**Answer.** 95°.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The chief advantage of a gyro-theodolite for underground orientation is that it
options: determines true (geographic) north independently of the earth's magnetic field, so it is unaffected by steelwork
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A spinning gyro seeks the meridian.

**Reasoning.** It finds true north from the earth's rotation, free of magnetic disturbance from rails, supports and cables, giving azimuth underground without long correlation traverses.

**Answer.** True north, immune to magnetic interference.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A magnetic compass is generally unreliable for underground mine surveying because
options: steel arches, rails, cables and machinery cause strong local attraction that deflects the needle
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Ferromagnetic material distorts the field.

**Reasoning.** Abundant steel and electrical equipment underground produce local attraction, making compass bearings erratic; theodolite traversing or gyro orientation is used instead.

**Answer.** Local attraction from steelwork.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a triangulation triangle, side a = 100 m lies opposite angle A = 30°, and angle B = 90°. The length of side b (opposite B), in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sine rule: $\dfrac{a}{\sin A}=\dfrac{b}{\sin B}$.

**Working.**
$$b=a\,\frac{\sin B}{\sin A}=100\times\frac{\sin90^{\circ}}{\sin30^{\circ}}=100\times\frac{1}{0.5}=200\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 200 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Triangulation as a method of horizontal control depends on measuring
options: the angles of a network of triangles together with at least one precisely measured baseline
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Angles plus a baseline fix the network.

**Reasoning.** With one measured baseline and the observed angles, the sine rule yields all other sides; redundant angles provide checks. It was the classic method for extensive control.

**Answer.** Angles + one measured baseline.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A 'well-conditioned' triangle in triangulation is one in which
options: no angle is too small or too large (angles roughly between 30° and 120°, ideally near 60°)
all sides are unequal and very different
it contains a right angle exactly
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Conditioning controls error propagation.

**Reasoning.** Computed side lengths are least sensitive to angular error when angles are moderate; very acute or very obtuse triangles magnify errors, so angles near 60° are preferred.

**Answer.** Angles ≈ 30°–120° (near equilateral).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The baseline in a triangulation system is
options: a line measured with high precision from which all other distances in the network are computed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The baseline scales the whole network.

**Reasoning.** Since only angles are observed elsewhere, the single precisely measured baseline provides the scale; an error in it propagates throughout, so it is measured with great care.

**Answer.** Precisely measured reference line.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: An angle is measured three times: 30°10′, 30°12′ and 30°14′. The most probable value is 30° m′; the minutes part m, in minutes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** For equal-weight observations the MPV is the arithmetic mean.

**Working.**
$$\bar{m}=\frac{10+12+14}{3}=12'\Rightarrow 30^{\circ}12'$$

**Answer.** 12′.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Which of the following are recognised classes of errors in surveying observations? (Select all that apply.)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Errors are gross, systematic or random.

**Reasoning.** Blunders are mistakes to be detected and removed; systematic errors follow a law and are cumulative (correctable); random errors are small, compensating and treated statistically. Magnetic declination is a known correction, not a class of error.

**Answer.** Gross, systematic and random.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A systematic error such as using a tape that is uniformly too long is characterised by being
options: of constant sign, cumulative, and removable by calibration/correction
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Systematic errors follow a definite law.

**Reasoning.** They have the same sign each time and accumulate with the number of measurements; once the cause is known (e.g. tape length) a correction removes them.

**Answer.** Constant sign, cumulative, correctable.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Random (accidental) errors in a set of observations are best reduced by
options: taking many observations and using their mean, since they tend to cancel
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Random errors obey the laws of probability.

**Reasoning.** Being equally likely positive or negative and small, they partly cancel; the mean of many observations is more precise, with the standard error decreasing as $1/\sqrt{n}$.

**Answer.** Average many observations.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A single observation has a standard deviation of 10 mm. The standard deviation of the mean of 4 independent observations, in mm, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\frac{10}{\sqrt{4}}=\frac{10}{2}=5\ \text{mm}$$

**Answer.** 5 mm.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Electronic distance measurement (EDM) determines distance by
options: measuring the travel time or phase difference of modulated electromagnetic waves between instrument and reflector
questions.solution:
**Concept.** EDM uses wave propagation.

**Reasoning.** A modulated light/infrared (or microwave) signal travels to a reflector and back; the phase shift or transit time gives the distance very accurately and quickly.

**Answer.** Phase/time of EM waves.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A total station is an instrument that combines
options: a level and a plumb bob only
an electronic theodolite (angles), an EDM (distances) and a microprocessor that computes coordinates
a GPS receiver and a barometer
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The total station integrates angle, distance and computation.

**Reasoning.** It measures horizontal and vertical angles and slope distance, then computes and stores horizontal distance, elevation and 3-D coordinates of the point, with on-board data recording.

**Answer.** Theodolite + EDM + processor.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A total station measures a slope distance of 100.0 m at a vertical angle of 30°. The horizontal distance, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $=$ slope distance $\times\cos(\text{vertical angle})$.

**Working.**
$$H=100.0\cos30^{\circ}=100.0\times0.8660=86.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 86.6 m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A GNSS/GPS receiver fixes its three-dimensional position by
options: trilateration from the ranges to at least four satellites of known position
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Satellite ranging by trilateration.

**Reasoning.** The receiver measures pseudo-ranges to several satellites; four are needed to solve for the three position coordinates plus the receiver clock error.

**Answer.** Trilateration from ≥4 satellites.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Differential GPS (DGPS / RTK) improves positioning accuracy by
options: using a reference (base) station at a known point to compute and broadcast corrections that the rover applies
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Correlated errors cancel between nearby receivers.

**Reasoning.** A base on a known point determines the range errors and sends corrections to the rover; common errors (orbit, clock, atmosphere) largely cancel, giving cm–dm accuracy (RTK to cm).

**Answer.** Base-station corrections to the rover.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A Geographic Information System (GIS) is primarily a system to
options: capture, store, analyse and display spatially referenced (geographic) data in layers
questions.solution:
**Concept.** GIS handles spatial data.

**Reasoning.** It integrates geometry with attributes in layers, enabling spatial query, overlay and analysis for mine planning, lease management, environmental and infrastructure studies.

**Answer.** Manage and analyse geographic data.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Which of the following are modern geomatics techniques used to obtain position or spatial data? (Select all that apply.)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Geomatics covers instruments and platforms that acquire spatial data.

**Reasoning.** Total stations, GNSS and photogrammetry/remote sensing all yield positional or spatial information. Bowditch's rule is a traverse-adjustment computation, not a data-acquisition technique.

**Answer.** Total station, GNSS, photogrammetry/remote sensing.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: For the closed traverse shown, the algebraic sums of latitudes and departures are $\sum L=+0.3$ m and $\sum D=-0.4$ m. The linear closing error, in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,160 160,160 180,80 90,40 30,90' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='40' cy='160' r='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='160' r='3'/><circle cx='180' cy='80' r='3'/><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='3'/><circle cx='30' cy='90' r='3'/></g><text x='32' y='176' font-size='10'>A</text><text x='158' y='176' font-size='10'>B</text><text x='186' y='82' font-size='10'>C</text><text x='86' y='34' font-size='10'>D</text><text x='14' y='92' font-size='10'>E</text></svg>
figure.caption: Closed traverse ABCDE

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closing error $e=\sqrt{(\sum L)^2+(\sum D)^2}$.

**Working.**
$$e=\sqrt{0.3^2+(-0.4)^2}=\sqrt{0.09+0.16}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.5\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.5$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In the levelling setup shown, the reduced level of A is 100.000 m, the back sight on A is 1.500 m and the fore sight on B is 2.300 m. The reduced level of B, in m, is _____ (three decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='140' x2='250' y2='140' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='130' y1='70' x2='130' y2='90' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='68' r='5' fill='#475569'/><text x='116' y='60' font-size='9'>level</text><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='40' y2='140' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='84' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>A</text><text x='44' y='112' font-size='9'>BS 1.5</text><line x1='220' y1='80' x2='220' y2='140' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='212' y='74' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>B</text><text x='176' y='112' font-size='9'>FS 2.3</text><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/></svg>
figure.caption: Differential levelling A to B

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A roadway rises 5 m over a horizontal distance of 100 m (see figure). Expressed as a gradient of 1 in $n$, the value of $n$ is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='120' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1'/><text x='204' y='96' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>rise 5 m</text><text x='70' y='135' font-size='10'>horizontal 100 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Roadway gradient

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gradient $1\text{ in }n$ means a rise of 1 unit per $n$ horizontal units, so $n=\dfrac{\text{horizontal}}{\text{rise}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{100}{5}=20$$

**Answer.** $1$ in $20$.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A total station measures a slope distance of 100 m to a target at a vertical angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal (see figure). The horizontal distance, in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='210' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='190' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='92' y='72' font-size='10' fill='#2563eb'>L = 100 m</text><path d='M72 120 A42 42 0 0 0 58 100' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='76' y='113' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>30°</text><line x1='190' y1='40' x2='190' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/></svg>
figure.caption: Slope distance and vertical angle

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $H=L\cos\theta$.

**Working.**
$$H=100\cos30^\circ=100\times0.8660=86.60\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $86.60$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A line has the whole-circle bearing of $210^\circ$ shown on the compass. Its reduced (quadrantal) bearing is

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='80' cy='80' r='60' fill='none' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='80' y1='15' x2='80' y2='145' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='15' y1='80' x2='145' y2='80' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><text x='74' y='12' font-size='10'>N</text><text x='74' y='156' font-size='10'>S</text><line x1='80' y1='80' x2='50' y2='132' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#bn2)'/><defs><marker id='bn2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#dc2626'/></marker></defs><text x='88' y='104' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>WCB 210°</text></svg>
figure.caption: Whole-circle bearing of a line

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For WCB between $180^\circ$ and $270^\circ$ (third quadrant) the reduced bearing is $S\,(\text{WCB}-180^\circ)\,W$.

**Reasoning.** $210^\circ-180^\circ=30^\circ$, so the line lies in the SW quadrant at $30^\circ$ from south.

**Answer.** $S\,30^\circ\,W$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A closed traverse has total error in latitude $\sum L = 0.3$ m and total error in departure $\sum D = 0.4$ m. Determine the closing (linear misclosure) error in metres.

figure.caption: Closed traverse with misclosure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,150 90,40 170,70 150,150' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#1f2937'><circle cx='30' cy='150' r='3'/><circle cx='90' cy='40' r='3'/><circle cx='170' cy='70' r='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='3'/></g><line x1='150' y1='150' x2='30' y2='150' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='20' y='172' font-size='8'>sumL=0.3 m, sumD=0.4 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The linear closing error of a traverse is the vector sum of the latitude and departure misclosures.

**Working.**
$$e=\sqrt{(\sum L)^2+(\sum D)^2}=\sqrt{0.3^2+0.4^2}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.5\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.5$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A traverse of perimeter $1000$ m has a linear closing error of $0.5$ m. Express the relative precision as a decimal fraction (error per unit length).

figure.caption: Traverse precision
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,130 100,30 180,70 140,130' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><text x='25' y='150' font-size='8'>perimeter=1000 m, e=0.5 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Relative precision $=$ closing error divided by total traverse length.

**Working.**
$$\text{precision}=\frac{e}{P}=\frac{0.5}{1000}=0.0005\ \left(=\tfrac{1}{2000}\right)$$

**Answer.** $0.0005$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: In a levelling run the reduced level of station $A$ is $100.000$ m, the back sight on $A$ is $1.500$ m and the fore sight on $B$ is $2.300$ m. Compute the reduced level of $B$ (m).

figure.caption: Differential levelling A to B
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='110' y='58' width='24' height='12' fill='#94a3b8'/><line x1='40' y1='64' x2='110' y2='64' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='134' y1='64' x2='200' y2='64' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='40' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><text x='34' y='34' font-size='8'>A</text><text x='194' y='34' font-size='8'>B</text><text x='18' y='146' font-size='8'>BS=1.5, FS=2.3, RL_A=100</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{RL}_B=\text{RL}_A+\text{BS}-\text{FS}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{RL}_B=100.000+1.500-2.300=99.200\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $99.2$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: With back sight $1.500$ m and fore sight $2.300$ m on the next point, find the magnitude of the rise or fall (m) between the two staff stations.

figure.caption: Rise and fall
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='40' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='30' y='146' font-size='8'>BS=1.5, FS=2.3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rise/fall $=\text{BS}-\text{FS}$; a negative value indicates a fall.

**Working.**
$$\text{BS}-\text{FS}=1.500-2.300=-0.800\ \text{m}\ (\text{fall})$$

**Answer.** $0.8$ m fall.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Two points are $100$ m apart horizontally and differ in level by $5$ m. The gradient is expressed as $1$ in $n$. Find $n$.

figure.caption: Gradient between two points
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='200' y1='60' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='95' y='125' font-size='8'>100 m</text><text x='205' y='90' font-size='8'>5 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gradient $1$ in $n$ means a rise of $1$ for every $n$ horizontal.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{\text{horizontal}}{\text{rise}}=\frac{100}{5}=20$$

**Answer.** $1$ in $20$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A simple circular curve of radius $200$ m has a deflection angle $\Delta = 60^\circ$. Compute the tangent length (m).

figure.caption: Simple circular curve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 A150 150 0 0 1 170 60' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='30' cy='150' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='170' cy='60' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='35' y='170' font-size='8'>R=200 m, deflection=60 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tangent length $T=R\tan(\Delta/2)$.

**Working.**
$$T=200\tan 30^\circ=200\times0.5774=115.47\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $115.47$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: For a circular curve of radius $200$ m and deflection $\Delta = 60^\circ$, find the length of the curve (m).

figure.caption: Length of circular curve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 A150 150 0 0 1 170 60' fill='none' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><text x='35' y='172' font-size='8'>R=200 m, delta=60 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Curve length $L=\dfrac{\pi R\Delta}{180}$.

**Working.**
$$L=\frac{\pi\times200\times60}{180}=209.44\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $209.44$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A circular curve has radius $200$ m and deflection angle $60^\circ$. Compute the length of the long chord (m).

figure.caption: Long chord of curve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 A150 150 0 0 1 170 60' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='35' y='172' font-size='8'>long chord, R=200, delta=60</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Long chord $LC=2R\sin(\Delta/2)$.

**Working.**
$$LC=2\times200\times\sin30^\circ=400\times0.5=200\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $200$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: For a curve of radius $200$ m and deflection $60^\circ$, compute the mid-ordinate (versine) in metres.

figure.caption: Mid-ordinate of curve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 A150 150 0 0 1 170 60' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='100' y1='105' x2='118' y2='86' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='35' y='172' font-size='8'>mid-ordinate M</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mid-ordinate $M=R\bigl(1-\cos(\Delta/2)\bigr)$.

**Working.**
$$M=200(1-\cos30^\circ)=200(1-0.8660)=26.79\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $26.79$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Compute the apex (external) distance for a circular curve of radius $200$ m and deflection angle $60^\circ$, in metres.

figure.caption: External (apex) distance
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 150 A150 150 0 0 1 170 60' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='118' y2='86' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='35' y='172' font-size='8'>external distance E</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** External distance $E=R\bigl(\sec(\Delta/2)-1\bigr)$.

**Working.**
$$E=200\left(\tfrac{1}{\cos30^\circ}-1\right)=200(1.1547-1)=30.94\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $30.94$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A tacheometer with multiplying constant $k=100$ and additive constant $c=0$ reads a staff intercept $s=0.500$ m on a horizontal sight. Find the horizontal distance (m).

figure.caption: Stadia tacheometry
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='160' y='30' width='10' height='100' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='160' y1='50' x2='170' y2='50' stroke='#dc2626'/><line x1='160' y1='110' x2='170' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626'/><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='12' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><line x1='52' y1='80' x2='160' y2='80' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='28' y='146' font-size='8'>k=100, c=0, s=0.5 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $D=k\,s+c$ for a horizontal stadia sight.

**Working.**
$$D=100\times0.500+0=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $50$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A stadia reading gives top and bottom hair readings of $1.750$ m and $1.100$ m. With $k=100$ and $c=0$, find the horizontal distance (m).

figure.caption: Stadia hair readings
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='160' y='25' width='10' height='110' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='158' y1='45' x2='172' y2='45' stroke='#dc2626'/><line x1='158' y1='110' x2='172' y2='110' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='28' y='146' font-size='8'>top=1.750, bottom=1.100</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Staff intercept $s=$ top $-$ bottom; then $D=ks$.

**Working.**
$$s=1.750-1.100=0.650\ \text{m};\quad D=100\times0.650=65\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $65$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A slope distance of $100$ m is measured along a line inclined at $30^\circ$ to the horizontal. Compute the horizontal distance (m).

figure.caption: Slope to horizontal reduction
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='95' y='95' font-size='8' transform='rotate(-25 95 95)'>L=100 m</text><text x='40' y='114' font-size='8'>30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $H=L\cos\theta$.

**Working.**
$$H=100\cos30^\circ=100\times0.8660=86.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $86.6$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: For the same slope distance of $100$ m at an inclination of $30^\circ$, compute the vertical component (difference in level) in metres.

figure.caption: Vertical component of slope line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='40' y='114' font-size='8'>30 deg</text><text x='205' y='85' font-size='8'>V</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Vertical component $V=L\sin\theta$.

**Working.**
$$V=100\sin30^\circ=100\times0.5=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $50$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Offsets $5, 7, 9, 7, 5$ m are taken at a regular interval of $10$ m along a baseline. Using the trapezoidal rule, compute the area (m$^2$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Trapezoidal rule: $A=d\left[\dfrac{o_0+o_n}{2}+\sum o_{\text{mid}}\right]$.

**Working.**
$$A=10\left[\frac{5+5}{2}+(7+9+7)\right]=10[5+23]=280\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** $280$ m$^2$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Offsets $2, 4, 6, 8, 10$ m are taken at $10$ m intervals. Using Simpson's one-third rule, compute the area (m$^2$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Simpson's rule (even intervals): $A=\dfrac{d}{3}\left[o_0+o_n+4\sum o_{\text{odd}}+2\sum o_{\text{even}}\right]$.

**Working.**
$$A=\frac{10}{3}\bigl[2+10+4(4+8)+2(6)\bigr]=\frac{10}{3}[12+48+12]=240\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** $240$ m$^2$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Two cross-sections $30$ m apart have areas $A_1 = 100$ m$^2$ and $A_2 = 140$ m$^2$. Using the end-area (average-area) method, compute the volume (m$^3$).

figure.caption: End-area volume between sections
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,90 70,50 90,90' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='160,95 210,40 235,95' fill='#cbd5e1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='30' y='115' font-size='8'>A1=100</text><text x='165' y='115' font-size='8'>A2=140, d=30 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** End-area method: $V=\dfrac{d}{2}(A_1+A_2)$.

**Working.**
$$V=\frac{30}{2}(100+140)=15\times240=3600\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** $3600$ m$^3$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: The fore bearing of a line is $70^\circ$ (whole-circle bearing). Compute its back bearing in degrees.

figure.caption: Fore and back bearing
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='40' y2='150' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='140' y2='55' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='-20' y2='111' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='25' font-size='8'>N</text><text x='90' y='62' font-size='8'>FB=70 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a whole-circle fore bearing below $180^\circ$, back bearing $=$ FB $+180^\circ$.

**Working.**
$$\text{BB}=70^\circ+180^\circ=250^\circ$$

**Answer.** $250^\circ$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Two survey lines meeting at a station have whole-circle bearings of $30^\circ$ and $90^\circ$. Compute the included angle between them (degrees).

figure.caption: Included angle from bearings
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='130' x2='50' y2='30' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='50' y1='130' x2='130' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='130' x2='150' y2='90' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='25' font-size='8'>N</text><text x='95' y='40' font-size='8'>30 deg</text><text x='120' y='80' font-size='8'>90 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Included angle $=$ difference of the two whole-circle bearings.

**Working.**
$$\theta=90^\circ-30^\circ=60^\circ$$

**Answer.** $60^\circ$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A line has a magnetic bearing of $45^\circ$ at a place where the magnetic declination is $2^\circ$ East. Compute the true bearing (degrees).

figure.caption: Magnetic and true north
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='60' y2='30' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='72' y2='32' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='25' font-size='8'>TN</text><text x='74' y='28' font-size='8'>MN</text><text x='100' y='95' font-size='8'>mag=45</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** With easterly declination, true bearing $=$ magnetic bearing $+$ declination.

**Working.**
$$\text{TB}=45^\circ+2^\circ=47^\circ$$

**Answer.** $47^\circ$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A traverse line of length $100$ m has a whole-circle bearing of $60^\circ$. Compute its latitude (m).

figure.caption: Latitude of a line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='40' y2='30' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='150' y2='140' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='135' y2='85' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='40' y2='85' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='105' font-size='8'>l=100</text><text x='52' y='124' font-size='8'>60 deg</text><text x='22' y='25' font-size='8'>N</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Latitude $L=l\cos\theta$.

**Working.**
$$L=100\cos60^\circ=100\times0.5=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $50$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: For the same traverse line ($100$ m, whole-circle bearing $60^\circ$), compute its departure (m).

figure.caption: Departure of a line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='40' y2='30' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='150' y2='140' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='135' y2='85' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='85' x2='135' y2='85' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='105' font-size='8'>l=100</text><text x='52' y='124' font-size='8'>60 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Departure $D=l\sin\theta$.

**Working.**
$$D=100\sin60^\circ=100\times0.8660=86.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $86.6$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Two plumb wires are hung down a vertical shaft to transfer surface orientation underground. The Weisbach triangle method is preferred because it:
options: requires the theodolite to be set exactly on the line of the two wires
allows the instrument to be set slightly off the wire line, with a small angle measured at the apex
eliminates the need to measure any horizontal angle
works only when the two wires are more than 10 m apart

figure.caption: Weisbach triangle in shaft plumbing
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='20' x2='60' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='130' y1='20' x2='130' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><circle cx='60' cy='40' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='130' cy='40' r='3' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='40' cy='130' r='3' fill='#dc2626'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='60' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='130' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='130' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb'/><text x='20' y='165' font-size='8'>instrument near the wires</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** In the Weisbach triangle the theodolite is set up nearly but not exactly on the line joining the two wires.

**Reasoning.** The small angle at the instrument (apex) is measured and, with the very short opposite side, the line direction is computed precisely using the sine rule, removing the impractical need to occupy the wire line exactly.

**Answer.** It allows the instrument to be set slightly off the wire line with a small apex angle measured.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: On the contour map shown, the contour lines are closely spaced in one zone and widely spaced in another. The closely spaced contours indicate:
options: an overhanging cliff that cannot be contoured

figure.caption: Contour spacing and slope
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='none' stroke='#92400e'><path d='M20 40 Q110 20 200 40'/><path d='M20 55 Q110 35 200 55'/><path d='M20 70 Q110 50 200 70'/><path d='M20 105 Q110 90 200 105'/><path d='M20 140 Q110 125 200 140'/></g><text x='30' y='154' font-size='8'>close spacing = ? slope</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Contour spacing is inversely related to ground slope at a fixed contour interval.

**Reasoning.** Where contours are close together the ground rises rapidly over a short horizontal distance, i.e. a steep slope; widely spaced contours mean a gentle slope.

**Answer.** A steep slope.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A total station integrates an electronic theodolite with an EDM. In a single pointing it can directly measure:
options: horizontal angle, vertical angle and slope distance

figure.caption: Total station measurement
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='55' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='105' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='66' y='60' width='28' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='94' y1='75' x2='170' y2='50' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><rect x='168' y='40' width='12' height='24' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='20' y='28' font-size='8'>theodolite + EDM to prism</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A total station combines angular and distance measurement electronically.

**Reasoning.** In one observation to a prism it records the horizontal angle, the vertical (zenith) angle and the slope distance, from which coordinates and reduced levels are computed on board.

**Answer.** Horizontal angle, vertical angle and slope distance.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A traverse leg has length $100\ \text{m}$ and a whole-circle bearing of $30^\circ$ from north (figure). Compute the departure (easting component) of the leg (m).

figure.caption: Traverse leg
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='140' y2='50' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='40' y2='50' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='140' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><text x='62' y='82' font-size='8'>L=100</text><text x='44' y='148' font-size='8'>bearing 30 deg from N</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Departure $=L\sin(\text{bearing})$.

**Working.**
$$\text{Dep}=100\sin30^\circ=100\times0.5=50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $50$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A line has a fore bearing of $60^\circ$ (figure). Compute its back bearing (degrees).

figure.caption: Fore bearing
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='90' cy='85' r='55' fill='none' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='90' y1='85' x2='90' y2='30' stroke='#1f2937'/><line x1='90' y1='85' x2='138' y2='113' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='95' y='28' font-size='8'>N</text><text x='28' y='162' font-size='8'>fore bearing 60 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Back bearing $=$ fore bearing $+180^\circ$ (when $<180^\circ$).

**Working.**
$$BB=60^\circ+180^\circ=240^\circ$$

**Answer.** $240^\circ$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A level is set up reading a backsight of $1.5\ \text{m}$ on a benchmark of RL $100.0\ \text{m}$, then a foresight of $0.8\ \text{m}$ on a point (figure). Compute the reduced level of the point (m).

figure.caption: Levelling between two points
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='100' y='40' width='20' height='10' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='45' x2='180' y2='45' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='40' y1='45' x2='40' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='180' y1='45' x2='180' y2='110' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='24' y='100' font-size='8'>BS 1.5</text><text x='158' y='100' font-size='8'>FS 0.8</text><text x='40' y='140' font-size='8'>BM RL 100</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $RL_{\text{point}}=RL_{\text{BM}}+BS-FS$.

**Working.**
$$RL=100.0+1.5-0.8=100.7\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $100.7$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Successive staff readings give a backsight of $2.0\ \text{m}$ and a foresight of $1.2\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the rise from the first point to the second (m).

figure.caption: Rise between readings
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='180' y2='40' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='40' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='180' y1='40' x2='180' y2='100' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='24' y='100' font-size='8'>BS 2.0</text><text x='158' y='90' font-size='8'>FS 1.2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rise $=BS-FS$ (positive means rising ground).

**Working.**
$$\text{Rise}=2.0-1.2=0.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.8$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Two stations $100\ \text{m}$ apart differ in level by $5\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the gradient (%).

figure.caption: Gradient between stations
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='200' y2='100' stroke='#475569'/><text x='42' y='118' font-size='8'>rise 5 m over 100 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gradient $=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{distance}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$g=\frac{5}{100}\times100=5\%$$

**Answer.** $5\%$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: On the map shown, the nested closed lines (figure) represent points of equal elevation. The constant elevation difference between adjacent lines is the:
options: contour interval, the constant vertical distance between successive contours
horizontal equivalent, the map distance between contours

figure.caption: Contour lines
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='none' stroke='#16a34a'><ellipse cx='100' cy='80' rx='70' ry='45'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='80' rx='48' ry='30'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='80' rx='26' ry='16'/></g><text x='38' y='142' font-size='8'>lines of equal elevation</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Contour definitions.

**Reasoning.** Each contour joins points of equal elevation; the fixed vertical difference between successive contours is the contour interval, distinct from the horizontal map spacing.

**Answer.** Contour interval, the constant vertical distance between successive contours.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A theodolite at horizontal distance $50\ \text{m}$ from a mast measures a vertical angle of $30^\circ$ to its top (figure). Compute the height of the top above instrument level (m, two decimals).

figure.caption: Vertical angle to a mast top
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='40' y2='50' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='180' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='180' y1='130' x2='40' y2='50' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='92' y='145' font-size='8'>D=50</text><text x='128' y='112' font-size='8'>angle 30 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $h=D\tan(\text{vertical angle})$.

**Working.**
$$h=50\tan30^\circ=50\times0.5774=28.87\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $28.87$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A closed traverse has a misclosure in easting of $0.3\ \text{m}$ and in northing of $0.4\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the linear closing error (m).

figure.caption: Traverse misclosure
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,130 160,130 130,40 50,60' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='130' x2='52' y2='118' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='3'/><text x='28' y='150' font-size='8'>dE=0.3, dN=0.4</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closing error $=\sqrt{\Delta E^2+\Delta N^2}$.

**Working.**
$$e=\sqrt{0.3^2+0.4^2}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.5\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.5$ m.
